title: Can Everyone Be Deceived All the Time?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy; the canonical Tamil chapter remains untouched. All seven source pages were checked, with particular attention to the newspaper quotations, dates, electricity figures, tariff comparisons, and closing signature. Clearly visible OCR character errors and accidental word breaks were corrected only in the reading copy. The source confirms the closing date as 27 September 2015.
paragraphs: From time to time, we have been pointing out how, for the past four and a half years, the AIADMK rulers have continued to deceive the people of Tamil Nadu by piling one statement contrary to the truth upon another about electricity generation and power cuts.

As evidence, I wish to cite what some recent newspapers have reported about these power cuts and interruptions. Yesterday, in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly, the Electricity Minister repeatedly claimed that there were no power cuts at all. But do you know what the English daily *The Times of India* reported yesterday morning?

> **POWER CUTS ARE BACK IN STATE AS WIND POWER GENERATION REDUCES.** > > **Shortage was 2400 MW in Last 2 days.** > > “Close on the heels of Tamil Nadu organising a Global Investors’ Meet with the promise of uninterrupted power supply to investors, the State has plunged into a serious power shortage. The shortage has been 2,400 MW in the last two days as wind power generation, which was about 2000 MW, has almost stopped in the last two days. Power demand hovered around 13,338 MW on Wednesday evening, whereas the supply was only 11,055 MW... Due to the shortage, many districts witnessed power cuts on Wednesday evening and Thursday morning.”

That is what it says. When this is the true position, if the Electricity Minister repeatedly tells the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly that there are no power cuts in Tamil Nadu, I wish to ask: can information so contrary to the truth be given in the Assembly?

Although most newspapers in Tamil Nadu concealed this state of affairs, *Dinakaran* carried a fairly detailed report on 25 September 2015 under the headline, “Two-hour power cuts return in many districts of Tamil Nadu; public stage midnight road block.” The report said:

> “Tamil Nadu requires an average of 13,940 MW of electricity each day. But only 10,400 to 10,500 MW is being generated. Consequently, between 500 and 600 MW had been purchased from private producers. With power generation in Tamil Nadu having declined considerably over the past few weeks, Electricity Board officials are said to have issued oral orders for two-hour power interruptions in most districts outside Chennai from the day before yesterday. > > “Previously, when a power shortage arose, Electricity Board officials would announce in advance which areas would face cuts, when they would occur, and how long they would last. But no announcement has now been made about these interruptions. In Kanyakumari district, power went off once every hour on the night before last. This caused great anger among Muslims preparing for the Bakrid festival. > > “Condemning the repeated power cuts, residents of the Azhagiyamandapam area gathered on the road with SDPI office-bearers at 8 p.m. and then attempted a road blockade. They dispersed after Thuckalay police held talks with them. At around 11 p.m., residents of Idalakudi in Nagercoil suddenly blocked the road. Electricity returned at midnight, after which they ended their protest and dispersed. Unannounced power cuts have also been imposed in Tirunelveli and Thoothukudi districts for the past four days.”

Why, on the 16th itself, an outage affected Tambaram town, Chitlapakkam Town Panchayat and part of Chromepet. When power had still not returned by midnight, the public surrounded the Electricity Board office in Nehru Nagar, Chitlapakkam, and dispersed only after officials pacified them.

Yet the Minister deceives us by saying there are no power cuts in Tamil Nadu. He deceives the Legislative Assembly. Why, he even deceives the Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu. Believing him, the Chief Minister declared at the Global Investors Meet on 9 September 2015: “Power cuts have been completely eliminated, and Tamil Nadu today stands as a power-surplus State.” If the investors who attended that conference understand, within a month of her declaration, what an enormous lie the Chief Minister's claim was, the rulers themselves must say how humiliating that would be for the Tamil Nadu Government.

Speaking at the same Investors Meet, Jayalalithaa said, “Tamil Nadu is the third-highest State in India in attracting foreign direct investment.” I am not misquoting her. The Tamil Nadu Government's Department of Information printed her speech as an attractive booklet. Yet within a few days of the Chief Minister's speech, the Union Government and the World Bank released a ranking of States conducive to industrial investment, prepared on the basis of ninety-eight criteria, in which Tamil Nadu stood twelfth.

Even *Dinamalar*, in a report titled “Why has Tamil Nadu fallen behind in the list of investment-friendly States?”, wrote that Tamil Nadu had no prescribed deadline for resolving investors' grievances and no provision for action against officials who failed to complete work within that deadline. Unlike Andhra Pradesh and Chhattisgarh, Tamil Nadu had no law guaranteeing that project approvals would be granted on time. Although the Government had announced “single-window clearance” in Tamil Nadu, it did not even have a dedicated website. Punjab and Andhra Pradesh offered a transparent facility through which applicants under the single-window system could track their applications; Tamil Nadu did not. The newspaper reported the matter at still greater length.
In the weekly *Ananda Vikatan*, Kanagaraj, a thinker belonging to the Communist Party of India (Marxist), said:

> “Foreign direct investment does not always arrive as promised. The image being created, as though some two and a half lakh crore rupees will come pouring in tomorrow morning, is not true. One important concession announced by the Tamil Nadu Government is that even if electricity is interrupted for three minutes, the Government will pay compensation. Is Tamil Nadu now a State with uninterrupted power? If uninterrupted electricity cannot be supplied, can one calculate the enormous compensation the Government would have to pay? > > “I am not making a sweeping statement merely to level an accusation. The Tamil Nadu Government's ‘Vision 2023’ envisaged a total investment of ₹15 lakh crore. Nearly one-third of it, ₹4.5 lakh crore, was to be invested in the electricity sector. Chief Minister Jayalalithaa announced three years ago that this would produce 20,000 MW of electricity and that 5,000 MW would be generated by 2017. The year 2017 is now within sight. But where is the electricity? No power-generation project has been properly implemented in the past three years. The reality is that they will be unable to produce even 500 MW by 2017. In these circumstances, with what confidence does the Tamil Nadu Government promise compensation if a power interruption occurs?”

These rulers implement no scheme. The one thing they tirelessly do is keep issuing announcements! And then the Assembly passes a resolution praising them merely for making an announcement! How many announcements have these rulers made about power generation during the past four and a half years? How many?

In particular, S.S. Subramaniam, General Secretary of the Tamil Nadu Electricity Workers' Central Organisation affiliated to CITU, wrote a full-page article about the electricity sector in *Theekkathir* on the 23rd. It described the power projects announced in the Assembly by these rulers under Rule 110 and explained in detail how those projects had still not begun and what condition they were in.
In a statement read under Rule 110 on 29 March 2012, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa announced:

> “I am delighted to inform this House that the Government under my leadership has now decided to implement a new project called the 660 MW Ennore Thermal Power Extension Project. This thermal-power project, to be commenced at an estimated cost of approximately ₹3,960 crore, will begin generation by the end of 2015.”
The end of 2015 has arrived. What happened to this project? Why has generation not begun?

In his speech yesterday, the Electricity Minister spoke at length about solar-power generation. On 20 October 2012, Jayalalithaa unveiled a plan to generate 3,000 MW through solar energy and announced that the Tamil Nadu Government planned to generate 1,000 MW in each of 2013, 2014 and 2015. But three years later, if the Minister tells the Assembly that a target has been set to install 3,000 MW of solar-power capacity by the end of 2015, what does that mean? Were the announcements of 1,000 MW in 2013 and another 1,000 MW in 2014 all lies?

An order issued by the Tamil Nadu Electricity Regulatory Commission on 12 September 2014 said that the Electricity Board could purchase electricity at ₹7.01 per unit from those who established solar-power stations and began generation by 12 September 2015. But the Commission extended this order by six months, until 1 April 2016. I asked at the time why this extension was granted. When solar electricity was available in other States for ₹5.40 a unit, why extend the period for purchasing it at the higher price of ₹7.01? Reports said that even the recent memorandum of understanding with the Adani Group decided upon a price of ₹7.01 a unit. When electricity was available outside the State at a lower price, why was an agreement made to purchase it at a higher one?

Although Mr Nagalsamy, one of the Commission's three members, opposed this decision at the time, was the order issued over his objection or not? When neither the Electricity Board, which would buy the power, nor electricity consumers had made any request, why did the Commission unilaterally extend this tariff order by another six months? Was it for Gujarat's Adani? The Electricity Regulatory Commission did not discuss this extension of the tariff order. It is said that buying electricity at this high contracted price for twenty-five years would cause a loss of ₹23,000 crore. What is the Electricity Minister's answer?

The Minister also claimed that purchase prices were higher in other States, citing Gujarat, Rajasthan, Maharashtra and Uttar Pradesh. But why did he remain silent about the same Adani Group agreeing to supply Madhya Pradesh with electricity at ₹6.04 a unit? Why did he conveniently conceal it?

Indeed, as early as 24 November 2013, *Dinamalar* carried a report under the headline, “Tamil Nadu's solar-power project is a washout.” It said that because the scheme contained numerous irregularities and Electricity Minister Natham Viswanathan showed no interest in attending to them, manufacturers of solar-energy equipment had decided to meet the Chief Minister and make a representation.

Taken as a whole, the Minister's reply says that there are presently no “power cuts,” only “power interruptions.” Through the technical label “power interruption,” they are cleverly trying to prove that the power cuts which continue in reality somehow do not exist. I have not offered my own verdict on whether power cuts exist; I have merely placed before you reports published in the newspapers.

Why, yesterday's *Kaalai Kathir* carried one report headlined, “Government Hospital plunged into darkness by power cut; patients cope by lighting candles,” and another headlined, “Three-hour power cut causes hardship; shortage of 2,170 MW.” If the Minister conceals everything happening in the State and gives the Assembly the false information that no power cut exists, will that lessen the suffering the people endure because of power cuts?

You may deceive some people for many days. You may deceive many people for a few days. But you cannot deceive everyone all the time! In the same way, the Minister will soon realise that the people of Tamil Nadu, who are coming to understand fully the misery of AIADMK rule, cannot be deceived forever. That day is near; it is not far away!
With affection,
M.K.
27 September 2015
